title: Cold-process lavender soap
subtitle: A 500 g oil batch at 5% superfat, scented with lavender
excerpt: A straightforward cold-process bar soap with an olive, coconut, and castor oil base, scented with Lavandula angustifolia essential oil. The batch makes roughly four 125 g bars after a 4-week cure.
sourceNotes: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), chapters 4-6: SAP values, water-as-percentage-of-oils lye discount, superfat calculation. SoapCalc lye calculator (soapcalc.net, accessed May 2026): per-oil NaOH SAP values used to derive lye weight. Bramble Berry beginner cold-process guide (brambleberry.com, accessed May 2026): temperature window and trace guidance.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly to 1 kg oil weight. Beyond that, work in separate 500 g batches rather than scaling the lye linearly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cured bar keeps 2 years in a cool, dark place on an open rack. Making well ahead improves the bar: 6 weeks gives a noticeably harder, milder result than 4.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years in a cool, dark place on an open rack, air circulating around each bar. Discard if the bar develops orange spots (DOS; the superfat oils have gone rancid) or smells sharp and paint-like rather than herbal.

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical reaction between a fat and an alkali (lye) that produces soap and glycerin. Each oil has a saponification value: the grams of sodium hydroxide needed to fully convert one gram of that oil into soap.
The percentage of oil left unreacted after saponification. A 5% superfat means 5% of the oil weight was not converted to soap. The result is a slightly richer, less drying bar.

The point in cold-process soap making when the batter thickens enough that a drizzle of batter leaves a brief trail on the surface before sinking. Light trace is the texture of warm custard; heavy trace is thick enough to hold a peak.

A reduction in the amount of water used to dissolve the lye, expressed as a percentage of total oil weight. A 33% lye discount means the water weight equals 33% of the oil weight. Lower water content speeds unmoulding and reduces the risk of soda ash.

content.text: Cold-process soap is bar soap made from scratch, combining plant oils with sodium hydroxide (lye) to produce soap and glycerin through

. This batch uses 500 g of oils, olive-heavy for a conditioning bar, with coconut for lather and castor to bind the bubbles. The essential oil is lavender at 3% of oil weight. The bars need 4 weeks to cure before use.

requires lye handling. Put on protective gloves and splash-rated goggles before opening the lye container. Add lye to water, never water to lye. The solution reaches 80-90°C instantly and releases fumes; work near an open window or outdoors.

items.prepNote: 60% of total oil weight. Provides conditioning, mildness, and a slow, stable lather.
30%. Hard at room temperature; melt before weighing if needed. Provides cleansing and large, fluffy bubbles.
10%. Binds lather, makes bubbles creamy and long-lasting.
Calculated for this oil blend at 5% superfat: (300 x 0.134 + 150 x 0.190 + 50 x 0.128) x 0.95 = 71.3 g, rounded to 71 g. Weigh precisely. Use 99% pure cosmetic-grade NaOH only.
33% of oil weight (lye discount). Use distilled, not tap water; minerals in tap water react with the lye and can cause spots.
3% of oil weight (approximately 15 ml). Lavandula angustifolia. Added at light trace.

content.text: Put on gloves and goggles. Weigh the distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Weigh the sodium hydroxide separately. Pour the lye into the water in a slow, steady stream, stirring as you go. Never add water to the lye. The solution heats to 80-90°C and releases fumes. Stir until the granules dissolve completely. Set aside to cool.

Weigh all three oils into a heatproof jug or pan. Melt the coconut oil if it is solid, then combine with the olive and castor oils. Heat gently to around 45°C if the oils are cold, or simply wait at room temperature if they are already liquid. The oils and lye solution both need to reach a temperature of 38-42°C before combining.

Pour the cooled lye solution into the oils in a slow stream, stirring as you pour. Use the stick blender in short 5-second bursts, alternating with stirring by hand. After 2-3 minutes of blending, the batter reaches
: the texture of warm custard, and a drizzle leaves a brief trail on the surface. Add the lavender essential oil and blend for a further 10-15 seconds until evenly incorporated.

Pour the batter into the silicone loaf mould. Tap the mould gently on the worktop to release any air. Cover with a piece of cardboard or a wooden lid, then wrap with a tea towel or old blanket. The insulation encourages the soap to enter gel phase, which gives a translucent, dense bar with better colour retention. Leave for 24-48 hours.

After 24-48 hours, test the soap by pressing a gloved fingertip into the surface. If it springs back without sticking, it is ready to unmould. Peel the mould away. Wear gloves at this stage: the soap is still alkaline and can irritate skin. Cut into four bars of roughly equal width with a sharp knife or a dedicated soap cutter. A 500 g oil batch fills a standard 1 kg silicone loaf to about 3 cm depth; four cuts gives bars around 3 cm wide.
Stand the bars on an open rack in a cool, dark, well-ventilated spot. The

oils in the bar continue to saponify slowly over the next 4 weeks, and excess water evaporates, making the bar harder and milder. Turn the bars at 2 weeks to even the drying. At 4 weeks, lick the edge of one bar: if it gives a faint tingle (the zap test), the bar needs more cure time. At 6 weeks it will be fully cured. The
in this recipe (water at 33% of oil weight) means the bars are typically ready at 4-6 weeks.

Keep cured bars on an open rack in a cool, dark cupboard with air circulating around them. A soap dish with drainage slots works well in the bathroom; a solid dish that holds water will shorten bar life considerably. The bar keeps 2 years or more from the cure date. Discard if orange spots appear on the surface (DOS, dreaded orange spots), which signal that the superfat oils have gone rancid. A sharp, paint-like smell rather than the original lavender scent is the same cue.
